name: plan
context: Preview infrastructure changes by computing the diff between Terraform configuration and real-world state
info: terraform plan reads the current state, refreshes it against live infrastructure, and computes what actions Terraform would take to make reality match your configuration. It never modifies infrastructure — it only produces a read-only execution plan showing resources to add, change, or destroy. Plans can be saved to a file with -out for later application via terraform apply, which is the recommended workflow for safety. The plan command supports three modes: normal (converge to config), destroy (tear down everything), and refresh-only (update state without changing infra). Targeted plans with -target focus on specific resources. The -detailed-exitcode flag returns exit code 2 when changes are detected, enabling CI gates. See [[terraform/init]] for initialization and [[terraform/apply]] for executing plans.
body:
# Terraform Plan

`terraform plan` computes an execution plan that shows what Terraform will do when you run `terraform apply`. It reads the current state, refreshes resource attributes from live infrastructure, and diffs the result against your configuration. No infrastructure is modified.

## Basic Usage

```bash
# Preview changes
terraform plan

# Save plan to file for later apply
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Plan with variable overrides
terraform plan -var='environment=staging' -var='instance_count=3'

# Plan using a variable file
terraform plan -var-file=prod.tfvars

# Plan destruction of all resources
terraform plan -destroy

# Refresh state only (no infra changes)
terraform plan -refresh-only
```

## Planning Modes

### Normal Mode (default)
Computes changes needed to converge real infrastructure to match configuration. This is the standard workflow.

### Destroy Mode (`-destroy`)
Plans the removal of every resource in state. Equivalent to running `terraform destroy` but only showing the plan without executing:

```bash
terraform plan -destroy -out=destroy.tfplan
terraform apply destroy.tfplan
```

### Refresh-Only Mode (`-refresh-only`)
Updates state to reflect external changes (manual console edits, drift) without planning any infrastructure modifications:

```bash
# Detect drift
terraform plan -refresh-only

# Apply drift reconciliation to state
terraform plan -refresh-only -out=refresh.tfplan
terraform apply refresh.tfplan
```

## Saved Plans

Always save plans in production workflows:

```bash
# Save the plan
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Apply the exact saved plan (no confirmation prompt)
terraform apply tfplan

# Clean up
rm tfplan
```

Saved plans capture the full configuration snapshot. The plan file is an opaque binary — do not name it with `.tf` extension. It may contain sensitive values in cleartext; treat it as a sensitive artifact.

## Targeting Specific Resources

Focus planning on individual resources and their dependencies:

```bash
# Plan changes to a single resource
terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web

# Plan changes to a module
terraform plan -target=module.vpc

# Plan replacement of a degraded resource
terraform plan -replace=aws_instance.web

# Multiple targets
terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web -target=aws_security_group.web
```

**Warning**: targeting should be used only for recovery or debugging. Regular use causes configuration drift because untargeted resources are ignored.

## Exit Codes with `-detailed-exitcode`

Useful in CI/CD pipelines to gate on whether changes exist:

```bash
terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=tfplan
EXIT_CODE=$?

case $EXIT_CODE in
  0) echo "No changes needed" ;;
  1) echo "Error during plan" ; exit 1 ;;
  2) echo "Changes detected — review and apply" ;;
esac
```

| Exit Code | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| 0 | Success, no changes |
| 1 | Error |
| 2 | Success, changes present |

## All Flags

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|----------|
| `-out=FILENAME` | Save plan to file for later `terraform apply` |
| `-var='KEY=VALUE'` | Set an input variable (repeatable) |
| `-var-file=FILE` | Load variables from a `.tfvars` file (repeatable) |
| `-target=ADDRESS` | Plan only this resource and its dependencies (repeatable) |
| `-replace=ADDRESS` | Force replacement of a resource (repeatable) |
| `-destroy` | Plan to destroy all resources |
| `-refresh-only` | Only update state from live infrastructure |
| `-refresh=false` | Skip state refresh (faster but may miss drift) |
| `-detailed-exitcode` | Return exit code 2 when changes are present |
| `-parallelism=N` | Max concurrent provider operations (default: 10) |
| `-input=false` | Disable interactive prompts |
| `-no-color` | Disable colored output |
| `-json` | Machine-readable JSON output |
| `-compact-warnings` | Show warning summaries only |
| `-lock=false` | Disable state locking (dangerous) |
| `-lock-timeout=DURATION` | Wait for state lock (e.g., `30s`) |

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `TF_VAR_<name>` | Set input variable `<name>` |
| `TF_CLI_ARGS_plan` | Extra arguments for every `terraform plan` invocation |
| `TF_IN_AUTOMATION` | Adjust output for CI/CD |
| `TF_INPUT` | Set to `0` to disable prompts |
| `TF_LOG` | Debug logging: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR |

## Reading Plan Output

Terraform plan output uses symbols to indicate actions:

```
  + create       — new resource will be created
  - destroy      — existing resource will be destroyed
  ~ update       — resource will be modified in-place
-/+ replace      — resource will be destroyed and recreated
  <= read        — data source will be read
```

The plan summary at the end shows totals:
```
Plan: 3 to add, 1 to change, 0 to destroy.
```

sections.title: Planning modes
sections.body: Normal mode (default) converges infrastructure to match configuration. Destroy mode (`-destroy`) plans removal of all managed resources. Refresh-only mode (`-refresh-only`) updates state to reflect external changes without modifying infrastructure. These modes are mutually exclusive. Destroy and refresh-only plans can be saved with `-out` and applied later.

sections.title: Saving and applying plans
sections.body: Use `-out=tfplan` to save an execution plan to a binary file. Apply it with `terraform apply tfplan` — this skips the confirmation prompt because the plan was already reviewed. Saved plans are the recommended workflow for production: plan, review, apply. Plan files contain sensitive data in cleartext and should be treated as secrets. Never name plan files with `.tf` extension.

sections.title: Targeted planning
sections.body: Use `-target=ADDRESS` to focus on specific resources (e.g., `aws_instance.web`, `module.vpc`). Terraform plans the targeted resource plus its dependencies. Use `-replace=ADDRESS` to force resource replacement even when no config change is detected. Targeting is for recovery and debugging only — regular use causes drift because non-targeted resources are silently skipped.

sections.title: CI/CD gating with exit codes
sections.body: Use `-detailed-exitcode` to gate CI/CD pipelines: exit 0 means no changes, exit 1 means error, exit 2 means changes detected. Combine with `-out` to save the plan and `-json` for machine-readable output. In GitHub Actions, check the exit code to decide whether to proceed to apply or skip.
